problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle and D be a point on the segment BC such that DC = 2BD. Let E be the mid-point of AC. Let AD and BE intersect in P.

Let two circles omega1 and omega2 intersect at points P and Q. A line through P meets omega1 again at A and omega2 again at B. Prove that angle AQB is independent of the line (it equals the angle between the circles).
Let ABC be a triangle. Let D, E, F be the reflections of a point P over the midpoints of BC, CA, AB. Prove that AD, BE, CF concur.
Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral. Let P = AB ∩ CD and Q = AD ∩ BC. Prove that the bisectors of angles at P and Q are perpendicular.
Two circles are internally tangent at a point T. A chord AB of the larger circle is tangent to the smaller circle at C. Prove that TC bisects angle ATB.
Let ABCD be a parallelogram. Let the bisectors of the four interior angles bound a quadrilateral. Prove that this quadrilateral is a rectangle.
Let ABC be a triangle with the incircle centred at I touching CA at E and AB at F. Let line EF meet line BC at point X. Prove that X, B, C form a harmonic range with the contact point on BC.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle and let P be a point on its circumcircle. Let M, N be the feet of perpendiculars from P to AB and AC. Construct the circle with diameter AP and show M, N lie on it.
Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral. Prove that the perpendicular bisectors of AB and of CD meet at the common circumcenter, which is equidistant from all four vertices.

Let two circles omega1 and omega2 intersect at P and Q. A line through P meets omega1 again at A and omega2 again at B. Prove that the measure of angle AQB does not depend on the choice of the line through P.
Let ABCD be a square with P the midpoint of side AB and Q the midpoint of side BC. Prove that lines DP and AQ are perpendicular.

Let ABC be a triangle. Build squares externally on sides AB and AC; let E be the outer vertex of the square on AB adjacent to A, and G the outer vertex of the square on AC adjacent to A, so that E = Rotate(B, -90 deg, A) and G = Rotate(C, 90 deg, A). Prove that BG = CE and that lines BG and CE are perpendicular.
Let ABCD be a parallelogram and M the midpoint of side CD. Line AM meets diagonal BD at P. Prove that P divides BD in the ratio BP:PD = 2:1.

Let ABC be a triangle inscribed in circle Gamma, and let M be the second intersection of the internal bisector of angle A with Gamma. Let F be the foot of the perpendicular from M to line AB. Prove that AF = (AB + AC)/2.

Let ABC be an acute triangle with altitudes BE and CF, where E lies on AC and F on AB, and let M be the midpoint of BC. Prove that ME = MF, and that segment EF is perpendicular to the line AO where O is the circumcenter.
Let ABC be a triangle with AB = AC. Let M be the midpoint of BC. Let P be a point on segment AM. Prove that PB = PC.
In triangle ABC, let I be the incenter and let the incircle touch BC at X. Let M be the midpoint of BC and let the line MI meet the altitude from A at point P. Prove that AP equals the inradius.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

problem_statement: Let ABCD be a parallelogram. A circle through A meets segments AB, AC, AD again at P, Q, R respectively. Prove that AP*AB + AR*AD = AQ*AC.

Let ABC be a triangle with circumcircle Gamma and let M be the midpoint of arc BC not containing A. The circle centered at M through B (and C) meets line AB again at P and line AC again at Q. Prove that A, P, Q form an isosceles configuration with PB = QC.
Let ABC be a triangle. Let P be a point and let A', B', C' be the reflections of P over the midpoints of BC, CA, AB. Prove that AA', BB', CC' concur at the reflection of P over the centroid.
Let ABCD be a square. Let M be the midpoint of side CD, and let the segments AM and BD meet at P. Prove that DP : PB = 1 : 2.

Let ABC be a triangle with a point D on segment BC. Let P and Q be the feet of the perpendiculars from D to lines AB and AC respectively, and let N be the midpoint of AD. Prove that P and Q lie on the circle centered at N with radius AD/2 (equivalently NP = NQ = AD/2), since angles APD and AQD are right angles.
Let ABCD be a square and P a point on side CD. The perpendicular bisector of AP meets side AD at Q. Prove that Q is equidistant from A and P, i.e. QA = QP.
Let ABC be a triangle. Points E and F lie on sides AC and AB respectively such that BF = CE. Let the circumcircles of triangles ABE and ACF meet again at point P (the Miquel-type point). Construct P.
Let ABC be a triangle inscribed in a circle. The tangent to the circle at A meets line BC at T. Prove that T is the pole; construct T and verify TA^2 = TB*TC by building the relevant segments.
Let ABCD be a parallelogram. Let M be the midpoint of side BC and N the midpoint of side CD. Prove that the segments AM and AN trisect the diagonal BD.

Let ABC be a triangle with circumcircle omega. Let D be the foot of the altitude from A onto BC. Let the perpendicular from D to AB and the perpendicular from D to AC have feet X and Y. Prove that B, X, Y, C are concyclic.

Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral whose diagonals meet at P. Let the circumcircle of triangle APD and the circumcircle of triangle BPC meet again at a point Q. Construct Q and show P, Q and the centre relations.

Let ABC be a triangle with AB < AC. The perpendicular bisector of BC meets the circumcircle at the arc midpoints. Let M be the midpoint of arc BC containing A. Let the line through M perpendicular to AB meet AB at a foot; construct the arc midpoint M and the incentre I.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle. Squares are erected externally on sides AB and AC, giving outer vertices. Let the squares on AB and AC have centres O1 and O2, and let M be the midpoint of BC. Prove that O1 M O2 is an isosceles right triangle; construct O1, O2, M.
Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral with AB parallel to CD (a trapezoid). Let the diagonals AC and BD meet at P. Let E be the midpoint of AB and F the midpoint of CD. Prove that E, P, F are collinear.

Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I. Let the line AI meet the circumcircle again at M (arc midpoint of BC). Let the circle centred at M through I meet line AB at point F and line AC at point E. Construct M, and the points where circle(M, I) meets the sides.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a right triangle with the right angle at C. Let CD be the altitude to the hypotenuse AB. Let the incircles of triangles ACD and BCD touch CD at points P and Q. Prove a relation; construct the two incircles' touch points on CD.

construction_steps.name: I1
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(A, C, D), AngleBisector(C, A, D))

construction_steps.name: I2
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, C, D), AngleBisector(C, B, D))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with AB = AC and apex angle at A. Let D be a point on side BC. The circle through A and D tangent to BC at D meets AB at E and AC at F. Construct this circle and the points E, F.

Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral with circumcircle omega. Let lines AB and CD meet at X, and lines AD and BC meet at Y. Prove that the circle with diameter XY is orthogonal to omega; construct X, Y and the segment XY.

Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I. Let the A-, B-, C-excircles touch BC, CA, AB at Na, Nb, Nc respectively (each excentre being the intersection of the internal bisector at its vertex with the external bisectors at the other two), and let Ng be the Nagel point, the common point of ANa, BNb, CNc. Let Sp be the Spieker centre, the incentre of the medial triangle of ABC. Prove that Sp is exactly the midpoint of the segment INg.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: Sp
construction_steps.label: Sp
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(Mb, Ma, Mc), AngleBisector(Ma, Mb, Mc))

construction_steps.name: Ia
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: Ib
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, B, A), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

problem_statement: Let three mutually disjoint circles be c1 = Circle(O1,1.5) with O1=(-4,0), c2 = Circle(O2,1.5) with O2=(4,0), c3 = Circle(O3,2) with O3=(0,4). The radical axis of a pair (ci,cj) is the line perpendicular to OiOj at the point Kij of equal power; the three radical axes meet at the radical center R, which lies outside all three circles. Let t be the tangent length from R to c1 and w = Circle(R,t). Prove that w is orthogonal to each of c1, c2, c3, i.e. |R Oi|² = t² + ri² for every i (w cuts each circle at right angles).
In triangle ABC, AB = 13, BC = 14, CA = 15. Let M be the midpoint of BC. Determine the length of the median AM. (Answer: AM = 2*sqrt(37) = 12.16552...)

Let ABC be a triangle with orthocentre H, incentre I, circumcentre O, the Bevan point V (reflection of I in O), and the de Longchamps point L (reflection of H in O). Prove that H, I, V and L are the vertices of a parallelogram, in particular that the segment HI is parallel and equal to the segment LV.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: H
construction_steps.label: H
construction_steps.command: Intersect(PerpendicularLine(A, Line(B, C)), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(C, A)))

problem_statement: Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I and Lemoine (symmedian) point K, where K is the common point of the three symmedians (each the reflection of a median in the corresponding internal angle bisector). Let Mt be the Mittenpunkt, the common point of the lines IaMa, IbMb, IcMc joining each excentre to the midpoint of the opposite side. Prove that the three points I, Mt and K are collinear.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: Ia
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(B, AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: Ib
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(C, B, A), PerpendicularLine(C, AngleBisector(B, C, A)))

construction_steps.name: K
construction_steps.label: K
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Reflect(Line(A, Ma), AngleBisector(B, A, C)), Reflect(Line(B, Mb), AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

problem_statement: Let c1 = Circle(O1,3) with O1=(-2,-1) and c2 = Circle(O2,3.2) with O2=(3,2) intersect at A and B. The angle of intersection of the two circles at a common point is the angle between their tangents there. Prove that the two circles meet at equal angles at A and at B: the angle ∠(O1 A, O2 A) between the radii at A equals the angle ∠(O1 B, O2 B) at B (equivalently the tangents make the same angle at both intersection points).
Triangle ABC has a right angle at A with AB = 6 and AC = 8. Let D be the foot of the perpendicular from A to the hypotenuse BC. Determine the length AD. (Answer: AD = 4.8)

Let c1 = Circle(O1,3) with O1=(-2,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,3.2) with O2=(3,1) meet at A and B, forming the orthodiagonal quadrilateral O1 A O2 B (a kite). Prove the area relation: area(O1 A O2 B) = (1/2)·|AB|·|O1O2|, the product of its two perpendicular diagonals halved.

Let ABC be a triangle with incentre I. The A-mixtilinear incircle is the circle tangent to side AB, tangent to side AC, and internally tangent to the circumcircle of ABC; its centre Oa lies on the internal bisector of angle A, and it touches AB at P and AC at Q. Prove that the incentre I is the midpoint of the chord of contact PQ.

construction_steps.name: I
construction_steps.label: I
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), AngleBisector(A, B, C))

construction_steps.name: P
construction_steps.label: P
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, B), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Q
construction_steps.label: Q
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Line(A, C), PerpendicularLine(I, AngleBisector(B, A, C)))

construction_steps.name: Oa
construction_steps.label: O_a
construction_steps.command: Intersect(AngleBisector(B, A, C), PerpendicularLine(P, Line(A, B)))

problem_statement: Let c1 = Circle(O1,1.5) with O1=(-4,0) and c2 = Circle(O2,3) with O2=(5,1). Let S be the external center of similitude of the two circles. For a point X on c1, let X' be the second intersection of line SX with c2. Prove that the homothety at S taking c1 to c2 sends X to X': the radii O1X and O2X' are parallel and SX'/SX = r2/r1 = 2.
In triangle ABC, AB = 7, BC = 8, CA = 9. Point D lies on segment BC with BD = 3. Determine the length AD. (Answer: AD = sqrt(46) = 6.78233...)

Let four lines AC, BD, AD, BC form a complete quadrilateral from A=(-6,0), B=(6,0), C=(2,5), D=(-1,-4), with E = AC ∩ BD and F = AD ∩ BC. The four triangles cut out are AED, EBC, ACF and DBF. Prove that the circumcircles of these four triangles pass through one common point (the Miquel point of the complete quadrilateral).

Let ABC be a triangle with circumcentre O. Let the first Brocard point Om1 (with angle Om1AB = angle Om1BC = angle Om1CA) and the second Brocard point Om2 (with angle Om2BA = angle Om2CB = angle Om2AC) be constructed by the tangent-circle method. Prove that the two Brocard points are equidistant from the circumcentre, i.e. OOm1 = OOm2.

construction_steps.name: cB
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(B, C))), A)

construction_steps.name: cC
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, Line(C, A))), B)

construction_steps.name: dA
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(A, B), PerpendicularLine(A, Line(A, C))), B)

construction_steps.name: dB
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(B, C), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(B, A))), C)

problem_statement: Let c = Circle(O,3) with O=(0,0) and let A=(3,0) be a point ON c. The polar of a point P with respect to c is the chord of contact of the tangents from P. Prove that as P approaches A along c the polar becomes the tangent at A; concretely, the polar line of the point A (taken on the circle) is exactly the tangent to c at A, which is perpendicular to the radius OA.
In triangle ABC with AB = 13, BC = 14, CA = 15, let the excircle opposite A touch line BC at T. Determine the radius of this excircle. (Answer: r_A = 12.)

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral. Trisect each side, marking the two trisection points on every side. Through the two trisection points flanking each vertex draw a line: line lB through the points on AB and BC nearest B, and similarly lC, lD, lA at the other vertices. These four lines bound a quadrilateral V1V2V3V4. Prove Wittenbauer's theorem: V1V2V3V4 is a parallelogram (V1V2 is parallel to V4V3 and V2V3 is parallel to V1V4).

Let ABC be a triangle with circumcentre O and Lemoine (symmedian) point K. Let Om1 and Om2 be the first and second Brocard points. Prove that the midpoint of the segment Om1Om2 (the Brocard midpoint) lies on the line OK.

construction_steps.name: K
construction_steps.label: K
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Reflect(Line(A, Midpoint(B, C)), AngleBisector(B, A, C)), Reflect(Line(B, Midpoint(C, A)), AngleBisector(A, B, C)))

construction_steps.name: cB
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(A, B), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(B, C))), A)

construction_steps.name: cC
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(B, C), PerpendicularLine(C, Line(C, A))), B)

construction_steps.name: dA
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(A, B), PerpendicularLine(A, Line(A, C))), B)

construction_steps.name: dB
construction_steps.command: Circle(Intersect(PerpendicularBisector(B, C), PerpendicularLine(B, Line(B, A))), C)

problem_statement: Let B=(-3,0) and C=(4,0). The Apollonius circle of B and C for ratio k=2 is the locus of points P with PB/PC = 2; its diameter endpoints are the internal point D (BD/DC=2) and external point E (BE/EC=2) on line BC. Let P be a point where this circle meets a transversal. Prove that PB = 2·PC and that angle DPE = 90° (so P lies on the circle with diameter DE).
In triangle ABC, angle A = 60 degrees, AB = 8, and AC = 5. Determine the length of BC. (Answer: BC = 7.)

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral. Let Ga, Gb, Gc, Gd be the centroids of triangles BCD, CDA, DAB, ABC respectively. Prove that the four lines A-Ga, B-Gb, C-Gc, D-Gd are concurrent at the centroid of the four vertices, G = (A+B+C+D)/4.
Let ABC be a triangle. Let the A-symmedian (the reflection of the median from A in the internal bisector of angle A) meet side BC at Ka. Prove that Ka divides BC in the ratio BKa : KaC = AB^2 : AC^2.

construction_steps.name: Ka
construction_steps.label: K_a
construction_steps.command: Intersect(Reflect(Line(A, Ma), AngleBisector(B, A, C)), Line(B, C))

problem_statement: Let c = Circle(O,3) with O=(0,0) and P=(5,1) outside it. The chord of contact of the tangents from P meets segment OP at Q. Prove that Q is the inverse of P in c: O, Q, P are collinear and OQ·OP = 9 (= r²); equivalently Q is the image of P under inversion in the circle c.
Two circles, each of radius 5, have centers O1 and O2 with O1O2 = 8. Determine the length of their common chord. (Answer: 6.)

Let ABCD be a convex quadrilateral. On each side erect a square externally with centres P (on AB), Q (on BC), R (on CD), S (on DA). Prove that the centroid of the four square-centres coincides with the centroid of the four original vertices: (P+Q+R+S)/4 = (A+B+C+D)/4.

Let ABC be a triangle. Let D be the point on BC with BD : DC = 1 : 2 and let E be the point on CA with CE : EA = 1 : 3. Let P be the intersection of AD and BE, and let F be the intersection of CP with AB (so that AD, BE, CF concur at P). Let D', E', F' be the isotomic reflections of D, E, F across the midpoints of BC, CA, AB respectively. Prove that the isotomic cevians AD', BE', CF' are also concurrent (the isotomic conjugate of P exists).
